title: Operators
description: Arithmetic, assignment, comparison, and logical operators, plus increment/decrement and the classic = vs == mistake.
objectives: Use comparison operators to produce a bool result

sections.text: We've already used a few operators without naming them: = for assignment, + inside age = age + 1;, and << with cout. An operator is a symbol that performs an operation on one or more values.
An operator is a symbol that performs an operation — such as arithmetic, comparison, or logic — on one or more values, called operands.

sections.code:
int a = 7;
int b = 2;

cout << a + b << endl; // 9
cout << a - b << endl; // 5
cout << a * b << endl; // 14
cout << a / b << endl; // 3  (integer division, see Type Casting)
cout << a % b << endl; // 1  (remainder of 7 / 2)

sections.text: % (modulus) gives the remainder of integer division. 7 % 2 is 1, because 7 divided by 2 is 3 with 1 left over. This becomes very common in DSA — checking even/odd, cycling through indices, and more.
Recall age = age + 1; from the Variables lesson. age += 1; means exactly the same thing, just shorter — a compound assignment combines an operation with an assignment in one step.
Every comparison produces a bool — printed as 1 or 0, exactly like we saw in Data Types. These will drive the if statements coming up next.

sections.code:
int a = 5; // assignment — sets a to 5

a == 5;    // comparison — checks whether a equals 5, produces true/false, changes nothing

sections.text: = assigns a value. == compares two values. Confusing them is one of the most common bugs in C-family languages — writing if (a = 5) instead of if (a == 5) silently assigns 5 to a and evaluates as true, instead of checking anything. We'll see exactly why this matters once we reach if statements.

rows: AND — true only if both sides are true
OR — true if at least one side is true
NOT — flips true to false, and false to true

sections.text: ++ increases a variable by 1; -- decreases it by 1. These will be everywhere once we reach loops.

a++ (post-increment) uses the current value first, then increments. ++b (pre-increment) increments first, then uses the new value. Both end with a variable that's one higher, but what gets printed or used in that exact statement differs.
Like ordinary math, * and / are evaluated before + and - unless parentheses say otherwise. When in doubt, parentheses make intent explicit: 2 + (3 * 4).

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing = when == is meant — if (a = 5) assigns instead of comparing, and is a classic hard-to-spot bug.
Forgetting that / between two ints truncates, and that % gives the remainder, not the quotient.
Mixing up && and || — && needs both sides true, || only needs one.
Confusing a++ and ++a — they both increment a, but differ in what value is used in the surrounding expression.
Assuming +, -, *, / all run strictly left to right — multiplication and division are evaluated before addition and subtraction.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between = and ==?
questions.answer: = is the assignment operator, setting a variable to a value; == is the comparison operator, checking whether two values are equal and producing a bool result.

questions.question: What does the % operator do?
questions.answer: It returns the remainder of integer division — for example, 7 % 2 is 1.

questions.question: What is the difference between a++ and ++a?
questions.answer: a++ (post-increment) uses the current value of a in the expression, then increments it afterward; ++a (pre-increment) increments a first, then uses the new value.

questions.question: What's the difference between && and ||?
questions.answer: && (AND) is true only when both operands are true; || (OR) is true when at least one operand is true.

questions.question: What does score += 5; do?
questions.answer: It's a compound assignment equivalent to score = score + 5;, adding 5 to score's current value and storing the result back into score.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: C++ operators include arithmetic (+, -, *, /, %), assignment and compound assignment (=, +=, -=), comparison (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=), and logical operators (&&, ||, !). A critical distinction is = for assignment versus == for comparison — mixing them up is a classic bug. Increment and decrement operators, ++ and --, come in pre and post forms: a++ uses the current value before incrementing, while ++a increments first. Comparisons and logical operators always produce a bool, which becomes essential once we reach conditional statements.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Operators
sections.text: = sets. == asks. % gives what's left over. && needs both. || needs one. a++ uses then adds. ++a adds then uses.
